revision.text:
'''長秋記'''（ちょうしゅうき）は、[[源師時]]の日記。
==書名由来== *「皇后宮権大夫」の官位にあったことから「皇后宮」の唐名「長秋宮」から。 *別名に「権大夫記」、名前から「師時記」、「水日記」など。
==概要== *源師時は[[有職故実]]等に明るい人物で、現在では[[院政]]期の朝儀典礼等の研究上不可欠の重要資料。 *当初、70巻ほどあったようだが欠巻があり現在は10数巻が確認されている。
==参考文献== *早稲田大学図書館総合書籍データベース「長秋記」http://www.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kotenseki/search.php?cndbn=%92%B7%8FH%8BL
==関連項目== *[[冷泉家時雨亭文庫]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:ちようしゆうき}} [[Category:日本文学]]

'''長秋記'''（ちょうしゅうき）は、[[源師時]]の日記。
==書名由来== *源師時が「皇后宮権大夫」の官位にあったことから、「皇后宮」の[[唐名]]「長秋宮」に由来する。 *別名に「権大夫記」、名前から「師時記」、「水日記」など。
==概要== *源師時は[[有職故実]]等に明るい人物で、現在では[[院政]]期の朝儀典礼等の研究上不可欠の重要資料。 *当初は70巻ほどあったようだが欠巻があり、現在は[[長治]]2年（[[1105年]]）から[[保延]]2年（[[1136年]]）までの期間の10数巻が確認されている。
==参考文献== *[http://www.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kotenseki/search.php?cndbn=%92%B7%8FH%8BL 早稲田大学図書館総合書籍データベース「長秋記]
==関連項目== *[[冷泉家時雨亭文庫]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:ちようしゆうき}} [[Category:日本文学]] [[Category:平安時代の日記]] {{Lit-substub}} {{Japanese-history-stub}}

'''長秋記'''（ちょうしゅうき）は、[[源師時]]の日記。
==書名由来== *源師時が「皇后宮権大夫」の官位にあったことから、「皇后宮」の[[唐名]]「長秋宮」に由来する。 *別名に「権大夫記」、名前から「師時記」、「水日記」など。
==概要== *源師時は[[有職故実]]等に明るい人物で、現在では[[院政]]期の朝儀典礼等の研究上不可欠の重要資料。 *当初は70巻ほどあったようだが欠巻が多くあり、現在は[[長治]]2年（[[1105年]]）から[[保延]]2年（[[1136年]]）までの期間の10数巻が確認されている。 *[[1134年]]（[[長承]]3年）1、2月の[[鳥羽院]]と待賢門院の熊野神社参詣に同行、参拝から京都帰洛までの詳細が記録されている。山伏の峰入の様や僧侶への供養米の配分など平安時代の神仏混淆の一端が伺え、参詣日程の調整など内部事情の細かな記録も多い。
==参考文献== *[http://www.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kotenseki/search.php?cndbn=%92%B7%8FH%8BL 早稲田大学図書館総合書籍データベース「長秋記]
==関連項目== *[[冷泉家時雨亭文庫]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:ちようしゆうき}} [[Category:日本文学]] [[Category:平安時代の日記]] {{Lit-substub}} {{Japanese-history-stub}}

==参考文献== *[http://www.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kotenseki/search.php?cndbn=%92%B7%8FH%8BL 早稲田大学図書館総合書籍データベース「長秋記]

revision.comment: Created page with "'''Chōshūki''' (長秋記) is the diary of [[Minamoto no Morotoki]]. == Origin of the Title == - The name ''Chōshūki'' is derived from Minamoto no Morotoki’s position as ''Kōgōgū Gon no Dainagon'' (皇后宮権大夫, Provisional Chief Councilor to the Empress Dowager). ''Chōshū'' (長秋) is the [[Chinese name]] for the ''Kōgōgū'' (皇后宮, Empress Dowager’s Palace). - Other names include ''Gon Dainagon-ki'' (権大夫記), ''Morotoki-ki'' (師時記)..."
revision.text:
'''Chōshūki''' (長秋記) is the diary of [[Minamoto no Morotoki]].
== Origin of the Title == - The name ''Chōshūki'' is derived from Minamoto no Morotoki’s position as ''Kōgōgū Gon no Dainagon'' (皇后宮権大夫, Provisional Chief Councilor to the Empress Dowager). ''Chōshū'' (長秋) is the [[Chinese name]] for the ''Kōgōgū'' (皇后宮, Empress Dowager’s Palace). - Other names include ''Gon Dainagon-ki'' (権大夫記), ''Morotoki-ki'' (師時記), and ''Mizu Nikki'' (水日記).
== Overview == - Minamoto no Morotoki was well-versed in [[yūsoku kojitsu]] (有職故実, court customs and precedents). Today, ''Chōshūki'' serves as an indispensable source for studying ceremonial and ritual practices during the [[Insei period]]. - Initially comprising around 70 volumes, many parts have been lost. Currently, approximately a dozen volumes remain, covering the period from [[1105]] (Chōji 2) to [[1136]] (Hōen 2). - Detailed records of the 1134 (Chōshō 3) [[Kumano pilgrimage]] by [[Emperor Toba]] (鳥羽院) and [[Fujiwara no Shōshi]] (藤原璋子, Taikenmon-in) are included, documenting events from the pilgrimage to the return to Kyoto. The diary provides insight into [[syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism]] during the [[Heian period]], including mountain ascetic practices, distribution of alms rice to monks, and logistical adjustments for the pilgrimage.
== References == - [https://www.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kotenseki/search.php?cndbn=%92%B7%8FH%8BL Waseda University Library Kotenseki Database - ''Chōshūki'']
== See Also == * [[Reizei Family Shiguretei Bunko]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:Chōshūki}} [[Category:Japanese literature]] [[Category:Heian diaries]] [[Category:12th-century books]] [[Category:Japanese Chinese classics]] [[Category:Emperor Toba]] {{Lit-substub}} {{Japanese-history-stub}}

'''Chōshūki''' (長秋記) is the diary of [[Minamoto no Morotoki]].
== Origin of the Title == - The name ''Chōshūki'' is derived from Minamoto no Morotoki’s position as ''Kōgōgū Gon no Dainagon'' (皇后宮権大夫, Provisional Chief Councilor to the Empress Dowager). ''Chōshū'' (長秋) is the [[Chinese name]] for the ''Kōgōgū'' (皇后宮, Empress Dowager’s Palace). - Other names include ''Gon Dainagon-ki'' (権大夫記), ''Morotoki-ki'' (師時記), and ''Mizu Nikki'' (水日記).
== Overview == - Minamoto no Morotoki was well-versed in [[yūsoku kojitsu]] (有職故実, court customs and precedents). Today, ''Chōshūki'' serves as an indispensable source for studying ceremonial and ritual practices during the [[Insei period]]. - Initially comprising around 70 volumes, many parts have been lost. Currently, approximately a dozen volumes remain, covering the period from [[1105]] (Chōji 2) to [[1136]] (Hōen 2). - Detailed records of the 1134 (Chōshō 3) [[Kumano pilgrimage]] by [[Emperor Toba]] (鳥羽院) and [[Fujiwara no Shōshi]] (藤原璋子, Taikenmon-in) are included, documenting events from the pilgrimage to the return to Kyoto. The diary provides insight into [[syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism]] during the [[Heian period]], including mountain ascetic practices, distribution of alms rice to monks, and logistical adjustments for the pilgrimage.
== References == - [https://www.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kotenseki/search.php?cndbn=%92%B7%8FH%8BL Waseda University Library Kotenseki Database - ''Chōshūki'']
== See Also == * [[Reizei Family Shiguretei Bunko]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:Chōshūki}} [[Category:Japanese literature]] [[Category:Heian diaries]] [[Category:12th-century books]] [[Category:Japanese Chinese classics]] [[Category:Emperor Toba]] [[Category:Ja]][[ja:長秋記]] {{Lit-substub}} {{Japanese-history-stub}}

'''Chōshūki''' (長秋記) is the diary of [[Minamoto no Morotoki]].
== Origin of the Title == - The name ''Chōshūki'' is derived from Minamoto no Morotoki’s position as ''Kōgōgū Gon no Dainagon'' (皇后宮権大夫, Provisional Chief Councilor to the Empress Dowager). ''Chōshū'' (長秋) is the [[Chinese name]] for the ''Kōgōgū'' (皇后宮, Empress Dowager’s Palace). - Other names include ''Gon Dainagon-ki'' (権大夫記), ''Morotoki-ki'' (師時記), and ''Mizu Nikki'' (水日記).
== Overview == - Minamoto no Morotoki was well-versed in [[yūsoku kojitsu]] (有職故実, court customs and precedents). Today, ''Chōshūki'' serves as an indispensable source for studying ceremonial and ritual practices during the [[Insei period]]. - Initially comprising around 70 volumes, many parts have been lost. Currently, approximately a dozen volumes remain, covering the period from [[1105]] (Chōji 2) to [[1136]] (Hōen 2). - Detailed records of the 1134 (Chōshō 3) [[Kumano pilgrimage]] by [[Emperor Toba]] (鳥羽院) and [[Fujiwara no Shōshi]] (藤原璋子, Taikenmon-in) are included, documenting events from the pilgrimage to the return to Kyoto. The diary provides insight into [[syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism]] during the [[Heian period]], including mountain ascetic practices, distribution of alms rice to monks, and logistical adjustments for the pilgrimage.
== References == - [https://www.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kotenseki/search.php?cndbn=%92%B7%8FH%8BL Waseda University Library Kotenseki Database - ''Chōshūki'']
== See Also == * [[Reizei Family Shiguretei Bunko]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:Chōshūki}} [[Category:Japanese literature]] [[Category:Heian diaries]] [[Category:12th-century books]] [[Category:Japanese Chinese classics]] [[Category:Emperor Toba]] [[Category:Ja]][[category:new pages]][[ja:長秋記]] {{Lit-substub}} {{Japanese-history-stub}}

'''Chōshūki''' (長秋記) is the diary of {{ill|Minamoto no Morotoki|ja|源師時}}.
==Origin of the Title== * The title originates from Minamoto no Morotoki's position as the "Kōgōgū no Gon no Dainagon" (Empress's Household Deputy Minister), with "Kōgōgū" (皇后宮) being referred to by its Chinese-style name "Chōshūgū" (長秋宮). * Alternative names include "Gon no Dainagon Diary" (権大夫記), "Morotoki Diary" (師時記), and "Water Diary" (水日記), derived from his name.
==Overview== * Minamoto no Morotoki was well-versed in {{ill|Yūsoku Kojitsu|ja|有職故実}} and other traditions, making this diary an indispensable resource for studying court ceremonies and protocols during the {{ill|insei|ja|院政}} period. * Initially consisting of about 70 volumes, many are now missing. Currently, around a dozen volumes covering the period from {{ill|Chōji|ja|長治}} 2 ({{ill|1105|ja|1105年}}) to {{ill|Hōen|ja|保延}} 2 ({{ill|1136|ja|1136年}}) have been confirmed. * In {{ill|1134|ja|1134年}} ({{ill|Chōshō|ja|長承}} 3), Minamoto no Morotoki accompanied {{ill|Emperor Toba|ja|鳥羽天皇}} (Toba-in) and {{ill|Fujiwara no Shōshi|ja|藤原璋子}} (Taikenmon-in) on their pilgrimage to Kumano. Detailed records describe their worship and return to Kyoto, shedding light on the {{ill|Syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏習合}} of the Heian period. These entries include observations on yamabushi entering sacred peaks, the distribution of rice offerings to monks, and detailed internal arrangements such as pilgrimage scheduling.
==References== * [https://www.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kotenseki/search.php?cndbn=%92%B7%8FH%8BL Waseda University Library Kotenseki Database: Chōshūki]
==See Also== * {{ill|Reizei Family Shiguretei Library|ja|冷泉家時雨亭文庫}}

'''Chōshūki''' (長秋記) is the diary of {{ill|Minamoto no Morotoki|ja|源師時}}.
==Origin of the Title== * The title originates from Minamoto no Morotoki's position as the "Kōgōgū no Gon no Dainagon" (Empress's Household Deputy Minister), with "Kōgōgū" (皇后宮) being referred to by its Chinese-style name "Chōshūgū" (長秋宮). * Alternative names include "Gon no Dainagon Diary" (権大夫記), "Morotoki Diary" (師時記), and "Water Diary" (水日記), derived from his name.
==Overview== * Minamoto no Morotoki was well-versed in {{ill|Yūsoku Kojitsu|ja|有職故実}} and other traditions, making this diary an indispensable resource for studying court ceremonies and protocols during the {{ill|insei|ja|院政}} period. * Initially consisting of about 70 volumes, many are now missing. Currently, around a dozen volumes covering the period from {{ill|Chōji|ja|長治}} 2 ({{ill|1105|ja|1105年}}) to {{ill|Hōen|ja|保延}} 2 ({{ill|1136|ja|1136年}}) have been confirmed. * In {{ill|1134|ja|1134年}} ({{ill|Chōshō|ja|長承}} 3), Minamoto no Morotoki accompanied {{ill|Emperor Toba|ja|鳥羽天皇}} (Toba-in) and {{ill|Fujiwara no Shōshi|ja|藤原璋子}} (Taikenmon-in) on their pilgrimage to Kumano. Detailed records describe their worship and return to Kyoto, shedding light on the {{ill|Syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏習合}} of the Heian period. These entries include observations on yamabushi entering sacred peaks, the distribution of rice offerings to monks, and detailed internal arrangements such as pilgrimage scheduling.

revision.text:
'''Chōshūki''' (長秋記) is the diary of {{ill|Minamoto no Morotoki|ja|源師時}}.
==Origin of the Title== * The title originates from Minamoto no Morotoki's position as the "Kōgōgū no Gon no Dainagon" (Empress's Household Deputy Minister), with "Kōgōgū" (皇后宮) being referred to by its Chinese-style name "Chōshūgū" (長秋宮). * Alternative names include "Gon no Dainagon Diary" (権大夫記), "Morotoki Diary" (師時記), and "Water Diary" (水日記), derived from his name.
==Overview== * Minamoto no Morotoki was well-versed in {{ill|Yūsoku Kojitsu|ja|有職故実}} and other traditions, making this diary an indispensable resource for studying court ceremonies and protocols during the {{ill|insei|ja|院政}} period. * Initially consisting of about 70 volumes, many are now missing. Currently, around a dozen volumes covering the period from {{ill|Chōji|ja|長治}} 2 ({{ill|1105|ja|1105年}}) to {{ill|Hōen|ja|保延}} 2 ({{ill|1136|ja|1136年}}) have been confirmed. * In {{ill|1134|ja|1134年}} ({{ill|Chōshō|ja|長承}} 3), Minamoto no Morotoki accompanied {{ill|Emperor Toba|ja|鳥羽天皇}} (Toba-in) and {{ill|Fujiwara no Shōshi|ja|藤原璋子}} (Taikenmon-in) on their pilgrimage to Kumano. Detailed records describe their worship and return to Kyoto, shedding light on the {{ill|Syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏習合}} of the Heian period. These entries include observations on yamabushi entering sacred peaks, the distribution of rice offerings to monks, and detailed internal arrangements such as pilgrimage scheduling.
==References== * [https://www.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kotenseki/search.php?cndbn=%92%B7%8FH%8BL Waseda University Library Kotenseki Database: Chōshūki]
==See Also== * {{ill|Reizei Family Shiguretei Library|ja|冷泉家時雨亭文庫}}

'''長秋記'''（ちょうしゅうき）は、[[源師時]]の日記。
==書名由来== *源師時が「皇后宮権大夫」の官位にあったことから、「皇后宮」の[[唐名]]「長秋宮」に由来する。 *別名に「権大夫記」、名前から「師時記」、「水日記」など。
==概要== *源師時は[[有職故実]]等に明るい人物で、現在では[[院政]]期の朝儀典礼等の研究上不可欠の重要資料。 *当初は70巻ほどあったようだが欠巻が多くあり、現在は[[長治]]2年（[[1105年]]）から[[保延]]2年（[[1136年]]）までの期間の10数巻が確認されている。 *[[1134年]]（[[長承]]3年）1、2月の[[鳥羽天皇|鳥羽院]]と[[藤原璋子|待賢門院]]の熊野参詣に同行、参拝から京都帰洛までの詳細が記録されている。山伏の峰入の様や僧侶への供養米の配分など平安時代の[[神仏習合|神仏混淆]]の一端がうかがえ、参詣日程の調整など内部事情の細かな記録も多い。
==参考文献== *[https://www.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kotenseki/search.php?cndbn=%92%B7%8FH%8BL 早稲田大学図書館総合書籍データベース「長秋記]
==関連項目== *[[冷泉家時雨亭文庫]] *[[東山御文庫]] {{DEFAULTSORT:ちようしゆうき}} [[Category:日本文学]] [[Category:平安時代の日記]] [[Category:12世紀の書籍]] [[Category:日本漢文の典籍]] [[Category:鳥羽天皇]] {{Lit-substub}} {{Japanese-history-stub}}

'''Chōshūki''' (長秋記) is the diary of {{ill|Minamoto no Morotoki|ja|源師時}}.
==Origin of the Title== * The title originates from Minamoto no Morotoki's position as the "Kōgōgū no Gon no Dainagon" (Empress's Household Deputy Minister), with "Kōgōgū" (皇后宮) being referred to by its Chinese-style name "Chōshūgū" (長秋宮). * Alternative names include "Gon no Dainagon Diary" (権大夫記), "Morotoki Diary" (師時記), and "Water Diary" (水日記), derived from his name.
==Overview== * Minamoto no Morotoki was well-versed in {{ill|Yūsoku Kojitsu|ja|有職故実}} and other traditions, making this diary an indispensable resource for studying court ceremonies and protocols during the {{ill|insei|ja|院政}} period. * Initially consisting of about 70 volumes, many are now missing. Currently, around a dozen volumes covering the period from {{ill|Chōji|ja|長治}} 2 ({{ill|1105|ja|1105年}}) to {{ill|Hōen|ja|保延}} 2 ({{ill|1136|ja|1136年}}) have been confirmed. * In {{ill|1134|ja|1134年}} ({{ill|Chōshō|ja|長承}} 3), Minamoto no Morotoki accompanied {{ill|Emperor Toba|ja|鳥羽天皇}} (Toba-in) and {{ill|Fujiwara no Shōshi|ja|藤原璋子}} (Taikenmon-in) on their pilgrimage to Kumano. Detailed records describe their worship and return to Kyoto, shedding light on the {{ill|Syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏習合}} of the Heian period. These entries include observations on yamabushi entering sacred peaks, the distribution of rice offerings to monks, and detailed internal arrangements such as pilgrimage scheduling.
==References== * [https://www.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kotenseki/search.php?cndbn=%92%B7%8FH%8BL Waseda University Library Kotenseki Database: Chōshūki]
==See Also== * {{ill|Reizei Family Shiguretei Library|ja|冷泉家時雨亭文庫}}

Currently, around a dozen volumes covering the period from {{ill|Chōji|ja|長治}} 2 ({{ill|1105|ja|1105年}}) to {{ill|Hōen|ja|保延}} 2 ({{ill|1136|ja|1136年}}) have been confirmed. * In {{ill|1134|ja|1134年}} ({{ill|Chōshō|ja|長承}} 3), Minamoto no Morotoki accompanied {{ill|Emperor Toba|ja|鳥羽天皇}} (Toba-in) and {{ill|Fujiwara no Shōshi|ja|藤原璋子}} (Taikenmon-in) on their pilgrimage to Kumano. Detailed records describe their worship and return to Kyoto, shedding light on the {{ill|Syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} of the Heian period. These entries include observations on yamabushi entering sacred peaks, the distribution of rice offerings to monks, and detailed internal arrangements such as pilgrimage scheduling.
==References== * [https://www.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kotenseki/search.php?cndbn=%92%B7%8FH%8BL Waseda University Library Kotenseki Database: Chōshūki]

'''Chōshūki''' (長秋記) is the diary of {{ill|Minamoto no Morotoki|ja|源師時}}.
==Origin of the Title== * The title originates from Minamoto no Morotoki's position as the "Kōgōgū no Gon no Dainagon" (Empress's Household Deputy Minister), with "Kōgōgū" (皇后宮) being referred to by its Chinese-style name "Chōshūgū" (長秋宮). * Alternative names include "Gon no Dainagon Diary" (権大夫記), "Morotoki Diary" (師時記), and "Water Diary" (水日記), derived from his name.
==Overview== * Minamoto no Morotoki was well-versed in {{ill|Yūsoku Kojitsu|ja|有職故実}} and other traditions, making this diary an indispensable resource for studying court ceremonies and protocols during the {{ill|insei|ja|院政}} period. * Initially consisting of about 70 volumes, many are now missing. Currently, around a dozen volumes covering the period from {{ill|Chōji|ja|長治}} 2 ({{ill|1105|ja|1105th year }}) to {{ill|Hōen|ja|保延}} 2 ({{ill|1136|ja|1136th year }}) have been confirmed. * In {{ill|1134|ja|1134th year }} ({{ill|Chōshō|ja|長承}} 3), Minamoto no Morotoki accompanied {{ill|Emperor Toba|ja|鳥羽天皇}} (Toba-in) and {{ill|Fujiwara no Shōshi|ja|藤原璋子}} (Taikenmon-in) on their pilgrimage to Kumano. Detailed records describe their worship and return to Kyoto, shedding light on the {{ill|Syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} of the Heian period. These entries include observations on yamabushi entering sacred peaks, the distribution of rice offerings to monks, and detailed internal arrangements such as pilgrimage scheduling.
==References== * [https://www.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kotenseki/search.php?cndbn=%92%B7%8FH%8BL Waseda University Library Kotenseki Database: Chōshūki]
==See Also== * {{ill|Reizei Family Shiguretei Library|ja|冷泉家時雨亭文庫}}

revision.text:
'''長秋記'''（ちょうしゅうき）は、[[源師時]]の日記。
==書名由来== *源師時が「皇后宮権大夫」の官位にあったことから、「皇后宮」の[[唐名]]「長秋宮」に由来する。 *別名に「権大夫記」、名前から「師時記」、「水日記」など。
==概要== *源師時は[[有職故実]]等に明るい人物で、現在では[[院政]]期の朝儀典礼等の研究上不可欠の重要資料。 *当初は70巻ほどあったようだが欠巻が多くあり、現在は[[長治]]2年（[[1105年]]）から[[保延]]2年（[[1136年]]）までの期間の10数巻が確認されている。 *[[1134年]]（[[長承]]3年）1、2月の[[鳥羽天皇|鳥羽院]]と[[藤原璋子|待賢門院]]の熊野参詣に同行、参拝から京都帰洛までの詳細が記録されている。山伏の峰入の様や僧侶への供養米の配分など平安時代の[[神仏習合|神仏混淆]]の一端がうかがえ、参詣日程の調整など内部事情の細かな記録も多い。
==参考文献== *[https://www.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kotenseki/search.php?cndbn=%92%B7%8FH%8BL 早稲田大学図書館総合書籍データベース「長秋記]
==関連項目== *[[冷泉家時雨亭文庫]] *[[東山御文庫]] {{DEFAULTSORT:ちようしゆうき}} [[Category:日本文学]] [[Category:平安時代の日記]] [[Category:12世紀の書籍]] [[Category:日本漢文の典籍]] [[Category:鳥羽天皇]] {{Lit-substub}} {{Japanese-history-stub}}

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[長秋記]] to [[Minamoto no Morotoki's Diary]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長秋記]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11653546]]
Wikidata name: Chōshūki
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Chōshūki''' (長秋記) is the diary of {{ill|Minamoto no Morotoki|ja|源師時|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Morotoki|qq=|1=Minamoto no Morotoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564068}}.

==Origin of the Title==
* The title originates from Minamoto no Morotoki's position as the "Kōgōgū no Gon no Dainagon" (Empress's Household Deputy Minister), with "Kōgōgū" (皇后宮) being referred to by its Chinese-style name "Chōshūgū" (長秋宮).
* Alternative names include "Gon no Dainagon Diary" (権大夫記), "Morotoki Diary" (師時記), and "Water Diary" (水日記), derived from his name.

==Overview==
* Minamoto no Morotoki was well-versed in {{ill|Yūsoku Kojitsu|ja|有職故実|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yūsoku Kojitsu|qq=}} and other traditions, making this diary an indispensable resource for studying court ceremonies and protocols during the [[:en:Cloistered rule|insei]] period.
* Initially consisting of about 70 volumes, many are now missing. Currently, around a dozen volumes covering the period from [[:en:Chōji|Chōji]] 2 ([[:en:1105|1105]]) to [[:en:Hōen|Hōen]] 2 ([[:en:1136|1136]]) have been confirmed.
* In [[:en:1134|1134]] ([[:en:Chōshō|Chōshō]] 3), Minamoto no Morotoki accompanied [[:en:Emperor Toba|Emperor Toba]] (Toba-in) and [[:en:Fujiwara no Tamako|Fujiwara no Shōshi]] (Taikenmon-in) on their pilgrimage to Kumano. Detailed records describe their worship and return to Kyoto, shedding light on the [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] of the Heian period. These entries include observations on yamabushi entering sacred peaks, the distribution of rice offerings to monks, and detailed internal arrangements such as pilgrimage scheduling.

==References==
* [https://www.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kotenseki/search.php?cndbn=%92%B7%8FH%8BL Waseda University Library Kotenseki Database: Chōshūki]

==See Also==
* {{ill|Reizei Family Shiguretei Library|ja|冷泉家時雨亭文庫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reizei Family Shiguretei Library|qq=|1=Reizei Family Shiguretei Library|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11395344}}
{{Lit-substub}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

'''Chōshūki''' (長秋記) is the diary of {{ill|Minamoto no Morotoki|ja|源師時|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Morotoki|1=Minamoto no Morotoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564068}}.

==Origin of the Title==
* The title originates from Minamoto no Morotoki's position as the "Kōgōgū no Gon no Dainagon" (Empress's Household Deputy Minister), with "Kōgōgū" (皇后宮) being referred to by its Chinese-style name "Chōshūgū" (長秋宮).
* Alternative names include "Gon no Dainagon Diary" (権大夫記), "Morotoki Diary" (師時記), and "Water Diary" (水日記), derived from his name.

==Overview==
* Minamoto no Morotoki was well-versed in {{ill|Yūsoku Kojitsu|ja|有職故実|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yūsoku Kojitsu|qq=}} and other traditions, making this diary an indispensable resource for studying court ceremonies and protocols during the [[:en:Cloistered rule|insei]] period.
* Initially consisting of about 70 volumes, many are now missing. Currently, around a dozen volumes covering the period from [[:en:Chōji|Chōji]] 2 ([[:en:1105|1105]]) to [[:en:Hōen|Hōen]] 2 ([[:en:1136|1136]]) have been confirmed.
* In [[:en:1134|1134]] ([[:en:Chōshō|Chōshō]] 3), Minamoto no Morotoki accompanied [[:en:Emperor Toba|Emperor Toba]] (Toba-in) and [[:en:Fujiwara no Tamako|Fujiwara no Shōshi]] (Taikenmon-in) on their pilgrimage to Kumano. Detailed records describe their worship and return to Kyoto, shedding light on the [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] of the Heian period. These entries include observations on yamabushi entering sacred peaks, the distribution of rice offerings to monks, and detailed internal arrangements such as pilgrimage scheduling.

==References==
* [https://www.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kotenseki/search.php?cndbn=%92%B7%8FH%8BL Waseda University Library Kotenseki Database: Chōshūki]

==See Also==
* {{ill|Reizei Family Shiguretei Library|ja|冷泉家時雨亭文庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reizei Family Shiguretei Library|1=Reizei Family Shiguretei Library|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11395344}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長秋記]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11653546]] Wikidata name: Chōshūki [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Chōshūki''' (長秋記) is the diary of {{ill|Minamoto no Morotoki|ja|源師時|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Morotoki|1=Minamoto no Morotoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564068}}.
==Origin of the Title== * The title originates from Minamoto no Morotoki's position as the "Kōgōgū no Gon no Dainagon" (Empress's Household Deputy Minister), with "Kōgōgū" (皇后宮) being referred to by its Chinese-style name "Chōshūgū" (長秋宮). * Alternative names include "Gon no Dainagon Diary" (権大夫記), "Morotoki Diary" (師時記), and "Water Diary" (水日記), derived from his name.
==Overview== * Minamoto no Morotoki was well-versed in {{ill|Yūsoku Kojitsu|ja|有職故実|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yūsoku Kojitsu|qq=}} and other traditions, making this diary an indispensable resource for studying court ceremonies and protocols during the [[:en:Cloistered rule|insei]] period. * Initially consisting of about 70 volumes, many are now missing. Currently, around a dozen volumes covering the period from [[:en:Chōji|Chōji]] 2 ([[:en:1105|1105]]) to [[:en:Hōen|Hōen]] 2 ([[:en:1136|1136]]) have been confirmed. * In [[:en:1134|1134]] ([[:en:Chōshō|Chōshō]] 3), Minamoto no Morotoki accompanied [[:en:Emperor Toba|Emperor Toba]] (Toba-in) and [[:en:Fujiwara no Tamako|Fujiwara no Shōshi]] (Taikenmon-in) on their pilgrimage to Kumano. Detailed records describe their worship and return to Kyoto, shedding light on the [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] of the Heian period. These entries include observations on yamabushi entering sacred peaks, the distribution of rice offerings to monks, and detailed internal arrangements such as pilgrimage scheduling.
==References== * [https://www.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kotenseki/search.php?cndbn=%92%B7%8FH%8BL Waseda University Library Kotenseki Database: Chōshūki]
==See Also== * {{ill|Reizei Family Shiguretei Library|ja|冷泉家時雨亭文庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reizei Family Shiguretei Library|1=Reizei Family Shiguretei Library|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11395344}} {{Lit-substub}} {{Japanese-history-stub}}
}}
{{Lit-substub}} {{Japanese-history-stub}}

Detailed records describe their worship and return to Kyoto, shedding light on the [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] of the Heian period. {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11653546]]
Wikidata name: Chōshūki
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Chōshūki''' (長秋記) is the diary of {{ill|Minamoto no Morotoki|ja|源師時|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Morotoki|1=Minamoto no Morotoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564068}}.

==Origin of the Title==
* The title originates from Minamoto no Morotoki's position as the "Kōgōgū no Gon no Dainagon" (Empress's Household Deputy Minister), with "Kōgōgū" (皇后宮) being referred to by its Chinese-style name "Chōshūgū" (長秋宮).
* Alternative names include "Gon no Dainagon Diary" (権大夫記), "Morotoki Diary" (師時記), and "Water Diary" (水日記), derived from his name.

==Overview==
* Minamoto no Morotoki was well-versed in {{ill|Yūsoku Kojitsu|ja|有職故実|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yūsoku Kojitsu|qq=}} and other traditions, making this diary an indispensable resource for studying court ceremonies and protocols during the [[:en:Cloistered rule|insei]] period.
* Initially consisting of about 70 volumes, many are now missing. Currently, around a dozen volumes covering the period from [[:en:Chōji|Chōji]] 2 ([[:en:1105|1105]]) to [[:en:Hōen|Hōen]] 2 ([[:en:1136|1136]]) have been confirmed.
* In [[:en:1134|1134]] ([[:en:Chōshō|Chōshō]] 3), Minamoto no Morotoki accompanied [[:en:Emperor Toba|Emperor Toba]] (Toba-in) and [[:en:Fujiwara no Tamako|Fujiwara no Shōshi]] (Taikenmon-in) on their pilgrimage to Kumano. Detailed records describe their worship and return to Kyoto, shedding light on the [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] of the Heian period. These entries include observations on yamabushi entering sacred peaks, the distribution of rice offerings to monks, and detailed internal arrangements such as pilgrimage scheduling.

==References==
* [https://www.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kotenseki/search.php?cndbn=%92%B7%8FH%8BL Waseda University Library Kotenseki Database: Chōshūki]

==See Also==
* {{ill|Reizei Family Shiguretei Library|ja|冷泉家時雨亭文庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reizei Family Shiguretei Library|1=Reizei Family Shiguretei Library|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11395344}}

==Origin of the Title==

* Minamoto no Morotoki was well-versed in {{ill|Yūsoku Kojitsu|ja|有職故実|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yūsoku Kojitsu|qq=}} and other traditions, making this diary an indispensable resource for studying court ceremonies and protocols during the [[:en:Cloistered rule|insei]] period.
* Initially consisting of about 70 volumes, many are now missing. Currently, around a dozen volumes covering the period from [[:en:Chōji|Chōji]] 2 ([[:en:1105|1105]]) to [[:en:Hōen|Hōen]] 2 ([[:en:1136|1136]]) have been confirmed.
* In [[:en:1134|1134]] ([[:en:Chōshō|Chōshō]] 3), Minamoto no Morotoki accompanied [[:en:Emperor Toba|Emperor Toba]] (Toba-in) and [[:en:Fujiwara no Tamako|Fujiwara no Shōshi]] (Taikenmon-in) on their pilgrimage to Kumano. * Minamoto no Morotoki was well-versed in {{ill|Yūsoku Kojitsu|ja|有職故実|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yūsoku Kojitsu|qq=}} and other traditions, making this diary an indispensable resource for studying court ceremonies and protocols during the [[:en:Cloistered rule|insei]] period.
* Initially consisting of about 70 volumes, many are now missing. Currently, around a dozen volumes covering the period from [[:en:Chōji|Chōji]] 2 ([[:en:1105|1105]]) to [[:en:Hōen|Hōen]] 2 ([[:en:1136|1136]]) have been confirmed.
* In [[:en:1134|1134]] ([[:en:Chōshō|Chōshō]] 3), Minamoto no Morotoki accompanied [[:en:Emperor Toba|Emperor Toba]] (Toba-in) and [[:en:Fujiwara no Tamako|Fujiwara no Shōshi]] (Taikenmon-in) on their pilgrimage to Kumano. Detailed records describe their worship and return to Kyoto, shedding light on the [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] of the Heian period. These entries include observations on yamabushi entering sacred peaks, the distribution of rice offerings to monks, and detailed internal arrangements such as pilgrimage scheduling.

Currently, around a dozen volumes covering the period from {{ill|Chōji|en|Chōji|WD=Q1150870|lt=Chōji}} 2 ({{ill|1105|en|1105|WD=Q13719|lt=1105}}) to {{ill|Hōen|en|Hōen|WD=Q1193929|lt=Hōen}} 2 ({{ill|1136|en|1136|WD=Q19599|lt=1136}}) have been confirmed. * In {{ill|1134|en|1134|WD=Q19590|lt=1134}} ({{ill|Chōshō|en|Chōshō|WD=Q1194264|lt=Chōshō}} 3), Minamoto no Morotoki accompanied {{ill|Emperor Toba|en|Emperor Toba|WD=Q349284|lt=Emperor Toba}} (Toba-in) and {{ill|Fujiwara no Tamako|en|Fujiwara no Tamako|WD=Q900258|lt=Fujiwara no Shōshi}} (Taikenmon-in) on their pilgrimage to Kumano. Detailed records describe their worship and return to Kyoto, shedding light on the {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|WD=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} of the Heian period. These entries include observations on yamabushi entering sacred peaks, the distribution of rice offerings to monks, and detailed internal arrangements such as pilgrimage scheduling.
==References== * [https://www.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kotenseki/search.php?cndbn=%92%B7%8FH%8BL Waseda University Library Kotenseki Database: Chōshūki]
==See Also== * {{ill|Reizei Family Shiguretei Library|ja|冷泉家時雨亭文庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reizei Family Shiguretei Library|1=Reizei Family Shiguretei Library|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11395344}} {{Lit-substub}} {{Japanese-history-stub}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長秋記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長秋記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長秋記]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11653546]] Wikidata name: Chōshūki [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Chōshūki''' (長秋記) is the diary of {{ill|Minamoto no Morotoki|ja|源師時|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Morotoki|1=Minamoto no Morotoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564068}}.
==Origin of the Title== * The title originates from Minamoto no Morotoki's position as the "Kōgōgū no Gon no Dainagon" (Empress's Household Deputy Minister), with "Kōgōgū" (皇后宮) being referred to by its Chinese-style name "Chōshūgū" (長秋宮). * Alternative names include "Gon no Dainagon Diary" (権大夫記), "Morotoki Diary" (師時記), and "Water Diary" (水日記), derived from his name.
==Overview== * Minamoto no Morotoki was well-versed in {{ill|Yūsoku Kojitsu|ja|有職故実|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yūsoku Kojitsu|qq=}} and other traditions, making this diary an indispensable resource for studying court ceremonies and protocols during the {{ill|Cloistered rule|en|Cloistered rule|WD=Q865416|lt=insei}} period. * Initially consisting of about 70 volumes, many are now missing. Currently, around a dozen volumes covering the period from {{ill|Chōji|en|Chōji|WD=Q1150870|lt=Chōji}} 2 ({{ill|1105|en|1105|WD=Q13719|lt=1105}}) to {{ill|Hōen|en|Hōen|WD=Q1193929|lt=Hōen}} 2 ({{ill|1136|en|1136|WD=Q19599|lt=1136}}) have been confirmed. * In {{ill|1134|en|1134|WD=Q19590|lt=1134}} ({{ill|Chōshō|en|Chōshō|WD=Q1194264|lt=Chōshō}} 3), Minamoto no Morotoki accompanied {{ill|Emperor Toba|en|Emperor Toba|WD=Q349284|lt=Emperor Toba}} (Toba-in) and {{ill|Fujiwara no Tamako|en|Fujiwara no Tamako|WD=Q900258|lt=Fujiwara no Shōshi}} (Taikenmon-in) on their pilgrimage to Kumano. Detailed records describe their worship and return to Kyoto, shedding light on the {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|WD=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} of the Heian period. These entries include observations on yamabushi entering sacred peaks, the distribution of rice offerings to monks, and detailed internal arrangements such as pilgrimage scheduling.
==References== * [https://www.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kotenseki/search.php?cndbn=%92%B7%8FH%8BL Waseda University Library Kotenseki Database: Chōshūki]
==See Also== * {{ill|Reizei Family Shiguretei Library|ja|冷泉家時雨亭文庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reizei Family Shiguretei Library|1=Reizei Family Shiguretei Library|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11395344}} {{Lit-substub}} {{Japanese-history-stub}}

'''Chōshūki''' (長秋記) is the diary of {{ill|Minamoto no Morotoki|ja|源師時|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Morotoki|1=Minamoto no Morotoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564068}}.
==Origin of the Title== * The title originates from Minamoto no Morotoki's position as the "Kōgōgū no Gon no Dainagon" (Empress's Household Deputy Minister), with "Kōgōgū" (皇后宮) being referred to by its Chinese-style name "Chōshūgū" (長秋宮). * Alternative names include "Gon no Dainagon Diary" (権大夫記), "Morotoki Diary" (師時記), and "Water Diary" (水日記), derived from his name.
==Overview== * Minamoto no Morotoki was well-versed in {{ill|Yūsoku Kojitsu|ja|有職故実|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yūsoku Kojitsu|qq=}} and other traditions, making this diary an indispensable resource for studying court ceremonies and protocols during the {{ill|Cloistered rule|en|Cloistered rule|WD=Q865416|lt=insei|1=Cloistered rule}} period. * Initially consisting of about 70 volumes, many are now missing. Currently, around a dozen volumes covering the period from {{ill|Chōji|en|Chōji|WD=Q1150870|lt=Chōji|1=Chōji}} 2 ({{ill|1105|en|1105|WD=Q13719|lt=1105|1=1105}}) to {{ill|Hōen|en|Hōen|WD=Q1193929|lt=Hōen|1=Hōen}} 2 ({{ill|1136|en|1136|WD=Q19599|lt=1136|1=1136}}) have been confirmed. * In {{ill|1134|en|1134|WD=Q19590|lt=1134|1=1134}} ({{ill|Chōshō|en|Chōshō|WD=Q1194264|lt=Chōshō|1=Chōshō}} 3), Minamoto no Morotoki accompanied {{ill|Emperor Toba|en|Emperor Toba|WD=Q349284|lt=Emperor Toba|1=Emperor Toba}} (Toba-in) and {{ill|Fujiwara no Tamako|en|Fujiwara no Tamako|WD=Q900258|lt=Fujiwara no Shōshi|1=Fujiwara no Tamako}} (Taikenmon-in) on their pilgrimage to Kumano. Detailed records describe their worship and return to Kyoto, shedding light on the {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|WD=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} of the Heian period. These entries include observations on yamabushi entering sacred peaks, the distribution of rice offerings to monks, and detailed internal arrangements such as pilgrimage scheduling.
==References== * [https://www.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kotenseki/search.php?cndbn=%92%B7%8FH%8BL Waseda University Library Kotenseki Database: Chōshūki]
==See Also== * {{ill|Reizei Family Shiguretei Library|ja|冷泉家時雨亭文庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reizei Family Shiguretei Library|1=Reizei Family Shiguretei Library|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11395344}} {{Lit-substub}} {{Japanese-history-stub}}
}}
{{Lit-substub}} {{Japanese-history-stub}}
